description: Social engineering attacks tricking users into revealing credentials or installing malware
statistics: 91% of cyberattacks begin with phishing email; $4.76B lost in 2022 (FBI IC3)

best_practices: passphrases (easier to remember, harder to crack)
hashing: Passwords should be stored hashed (bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt) — never plaintext
breaches: Have I Been Pwned monitors 12B+ breached credentials

description: Malware encrypting victim's data and demanding cryptocurrency ransom for decryption key
trends: Double extortion (encrypt + threaten to publish); ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) model proliferating

description: Security model: 'never trust, always verify' — no implicit trust for any user or device
vs_perimeter: Traditional: castle-and-moat (trust inside network); Zero Trust: no inside/outside distinction
drivers: Cloud adoption, remote work, supply chain attacks make perimeter security obsolete

key_laws.GDPR (EU 2018): strongest global standard; fines up to €20M or 4% global revenue

key_laws.CCPA (California 2020): right to know, delete, opt-out; $7,500 per intentional violation

trends: 70+ countries now have data protection laws; US federal privacy law under development

description: Psychological manipulation exploiting human behavior rather than technical vulnerabilities
famous_cases: Twitter 2020 hack via phone spear phishing
RSA SecurID breach via Flash exploit phishing
Kevin Mitnick (world's most famous social engineer)

description: Process of encoding information so only authorized parties can read it

types.symmetric: same key encrypts/decrypts (AES-256); fast; key distribution challenge

types.asymmetric: public/private key pair (RSA, ECC); solves key distribution; slower

quantum_threat: Quantum computers could break RSA/ECC; NIST post-quantum standards finalized 2024 (CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium)
end_to_end: Provider cannot read messages; even legal requests cannot access content
